data.title: Docebo: FY 2026 Looks Better, But Land-And-Expand Still Needs To Prove Itself
data.description:
Investment Summary

Docebo (DCBO) is a cloud-based enterprise learning platform. It is used to train employees as well as customers, partners, franchisees. It has expanded beyond its original LMS scope into AI content creation, analytics, training, as well as enterprise knowledge and workforce skills intelligence.

Docebo is repositioning strongly with larger enterprise clients, which should ultimately mean better retention and larger ARR per client. Its recent inclusion of skills intelligence, notably through the acquisition of 365Talents, is also positive because it makes their existing offering more complete and responds to a demand for a more practical and dynamic learning experience.

The question now is whether the move to larger accounts and the addition of skills intelligence can push NDR above 100% and support durable ARR acceleration. So far, we are not yet upgrading the stock to a Buy rating. FY25 still showed limited growth and relatively weak retention, while FY26 guidance does not yet point to a strong enough acceleration to justify a more aggressive stance. As of today, the stock looks fairly priced rather than clearly mispriced.

Our rating is: Hold/Watchlist.

Business Overview

Docebo's training/skills platform is used internally for onboarding, compliance, professional development, and sales training. They also provide learning tools for external purposes, such as certifications, franchise training, membership training, or customer academies. Management said nearly 50% of its ARR is from customers using Docebo for both internal and external training.

The business generates revenue mostly through annual and multi-year subscription contracts. Its pricing is based on factors such as the number of active or registered users, minimum user commitments, and selected modules. On top of those, Docebo brings an array of extra modules through various integrations with platforms such as Salesforce or Microsoft Teams for clients who need such use cases.

This external training angle is significant because it allows Docebo to go after a much bigger opportunity than the HR training market alone:

What The Market Thinks

The market is skeptical about the fact that Docebo's growth has slowed significantly from the post-Covid years, as in FY25 ARR only grew 8.4%, subscription revenue growth slowed, and NDR fell to 99%. The long-term trajectory of the ARR could explain the lack of momentum for the stock and the market's unwillingness to underwrite a premium SaaS multiple, as even with Q1/26's potential improvement, the long-term trend remains one of deceleration:

The Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Dayforce (recently acquired by Thoma Bravo) churn have hurt the story and continue to somewhat hurt optics. AWS appears to have brought its Amazon Skills Builder initiative in-house, causing it to disengage from Docebo in Q4/25, and Dayforce began winding down its OEM relationship with Docebo after acquiring eloomi in 2024 (its LMS asset). The largest OEM (understood to be Dayforce based on management's comments) appears to have represented 9.5% of ARR at year-end 2024 and 4.4% at year-end 2025. Management expects the largest OEM to contribute about 3-4% of FY26 revenue before becoming much less significant thereafter.

The bear case relies on this simple fact: if the installed base is not expanding as reflected by a 99% NDR, and if the largest corporate clients can internalize major learning use cases, with competitors offering similar AI-based skills features, then Docebo may still be perceived as a mature LMS vendor, which should cap its valuation potential.

What The Numbers Actually Show

The numbers are mixed but not bad. Revenue growth is not really exciting, with FY25 ARR reaching ~$238.1M (up only 8.4%). NDR at 99% shows that Docebo is also far from a world-class land-and-expand software yet. Also, the ~$13.1M tax recovery driven by changes in deferred tax assets and unrecognized losses also boosted FY25 net income to ~$37.5M. This makes the GAAP P/E TTM look cheaper than normalized earnings would suggest.

The investment case recently improved with Docebo's management announcing a stronger Q1/26 and raising FY26 guidance for revenue and adjusted EBITDA. It does not prove a full reacceleration yet but shows that FY26 is starting better than initially expected:

Another very positive fact is that FY25 adjusted EBITDA was ~$43.9M, up from ~$33.6M in FY24, while adjusted EBITDA margin was ~18.1%. This is clear operating leverage:

Last but not least, SBC was only roughly ~$6M in FY25, which is very reasonable for a SaaS business generating close to $250M of revenue per year (roughly 2%):

Docebo's preliminary Q1/26 results are indeed suggesting early signs of reacceleration. The expected revenue growth is pointed at 14.3%, with ARR growth of 10.6% and adjusted EBITDA growth of 22.5%. If one excludes the largest OEM customer, FX headwinds, and the acquired ARR, then ARR would have increased by roughly 13.7%, faster than the headline figure implies. These figures are unaudited and should be treated cautiously for now.

The Decisive Variable

The question is not whether Docebo has a credible AI story, but rather whether the business can improve its NDR and keep growing its ARR figure after the Dayforce/AWS-related revenue declines are largely absorbed (Q4/25 for AWS, likely FY26 or FY27 for Dayforce).

The second driver is strategic. Docebo is moving to larger accounts where customers are spending more and apparently have better retention. The April investor presentation mentioned that large enterprise accounts (those with ARR over $100K) have a 9-point higher three year average NDRR compared to customers generating less than $50K ARR:

The third driver is product completeness. Management said that in FY25 enterprise client targets wanted skills-intelligence features, and Docebo was falling short on those requirements. This explains why they acquired 365Talents to fill that gap. That said, the latter is not likely to change financials immediately, as this acquisition should only bring about around $9M of additional revenue. In the longer term, though, that base could grow fast and help Docebo win larger LMS deals, as well as possibly improve its retention figure with a wider and more complete set of features needed by the market.

What Would Change Our Thesis

What would make us more constructive on the stock would be to see consecutive organic ARR acceleration. The additional ARR brought by 365Talents is valuable and will complete Docebo's existing product suite but came at a cost of $54.6M with up to $5.1M contingent consideration. For now, 365Talents remains a small add-on to Docebo's platform, so we would need to see further proof that the former can generate the 30% ARR growth management is expecting.

We would like to see NDR moving decisively above the 100% threshold toward the 110% to 120% level that elite SaaS typically have. This would in turn bring us more confidence that Docebo can apply an effective land-and-expand strategy within existing accounts that we do not yet see.

Another positive sign that margins are durable would be to see adjusted EBITDA margin remaining close to 20% without gross margin pressure from AI compute, integration complexity, or third-party costs. We also want to observe whether other large customers would follow AWS's route to create in-house platforms for their own staff.

Valuation

Company Business Type EV/Sales TTM EV/EBITDA TTM Growth YoY
Docebo (DCBO) Enterprise LMS/workforce readiness ~1.9x ~14.6x ~11.8%
Coursera (COUR) Online learning/enterprise learning ~0.25x N/A ~9.8%
Udemy (UDMY) Online learning/enterprise up-skilling ~0.5x ~12x ~0.4%
Workday (WDAY) HCM/finance enterprise SaaS ~3.0x ~20.9x ~13.1%

Looking at the valuation table above, Docebo seems quite cheap on EV/sales but not so much on normalized earnings. Looking at comps with similar growth profiles, a 14x to 15x EV/EBITDA multiple seems reasonable for a profitable EdTech but not necessarily cheap for a SaaS company with <100% NDR and high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth.

Again, the current P/E TTM figure should be understood in the context of FY25 having benefited from a significant tax recovery, which makes the GAAP P/E figure too flattering if one assesses the business on a normalized basis.

Until Docebo can prove that it can achieve a normalized ARR growth closer to mid-teens and NDR improves significantly above 100%, there is no need to underwrite a premium multiple for the stock. The stock seems fairly valued at today's price.

Conclusion

Docebo is a good business but not necessarily a compelling stock as of today. The company improved its financials and positioning entering into FY26.

Docebo's AI workforce readiness narrative seems credible as Docebo goes beyond a simple chatbot by trying to combine learning, knowledge, skills, compliance features, and external training data to make its enterprise platform more useful and comprehensive.

That said, the decisive variable remains unproven so far as Docebo needs to show that it can accelerate its ARR in a durable and organic way, with NDR improving for a land-and-expand strategy to be effective and credible. Otherwise, the stock likely does not deserve an aggressive rating based on where its closest peer sets are trading at.

Docebo is worth monitoring closely but not yet exciting enough to warrant a Buy rating. For now, we believe this is a Hold and we will keep it on our watchlist.

data.title: East West Bancorp: Quality Remains, Upside Limited
data.description:
Introduction

In my view, East West Bancorp remains a very good quality and well-managed bank, which is demonstrating strong profitability, disciplined risk control, and also one of the most efficient cost structures in the sector.

East West Bancorp (EWBC) takes up a specific niche as the main financial bridge between the US and the Greater Chinese region, focusing on commercial banking and international services. Currently, the bank is showing a high operational efficiency, which is backed by the 2026 Q1 results. The net income reached $358 million, and EPS was at $2.57, recording a 23% yearly growth.

The total revenues increased 12% to $774 million, and the net interest margin expanded to 3.49%, compared to 3.35% last year. This increase is showing the bank's ability to optimize financing costs when the total cost of deposits dropped to 2.10%. The bank's loan portfolio also reached a record level of $58.1 billion, while deposits grew by 9% to $68.9 billion.

Especially important is the 12% growth of fee income, which reached $99 million and was driven by asset management services. The efficiency ratio is reaching 36.2%, which suggests a very low-cost structure compared to the regional bank average. The current P/E of 12.5x is above the three-year average of this company, showing that the market has already priced in these positive results, and shares are not cheap anymore. The capital base remains strong with the 15.13% CET1 ratio, allowing it to continue capital return to shareholders through the $0.80 dividend and share buybacks. Credit quality also remains stable, and net write-offs only make up 0.09% of the loan portfolio, which lowers the risk profile during an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

Business overview

East West Bancorp's business model is based on specialized commercial banking, servicing the US and Greater China's trade flows. The bank's revenue structure is showing that a product rotation is happening with a goal to diversify the risk and stabilize the revenues. In the loan portfolio, which is reaching $58.1 billion, a shift to commercial and industrial loans can be seen, which in the year grew 8% to $21.5 billion.

However, the commercial real estate segment grew a bit slower at just 3% to $20.2 billion, which is showing a conscious risk reduction in the most vulnerable office and retail sectors. The deposit structure is also having a rotation from non-interest-bearing current accounts to interest-bearing accounts and term deposits. The NCA part in the overall $68.9 billion deposit portfolio shrank to 28%, compared to 32% a year earlier, which reduced the base of cheap funding, while average financing costs stood at 2.10%. Though this change ensures deposit base stability in the competitive environment, revenue rotation is also seen in the non-interest revenue segment, which reached a record level of $99 million.

The asset management and foreign exchange operations commission fees grew 15%, compensating for possible fluctuations in interest income. Such product dynamics show the bank's efforts to lower the dependency on the net interest margin, which, even though it remains high at 3.49%, is still sensitive to monetary policy changes. An effective capital distribution between the less risky commercial and industrial loans and also the growing service fees is strengthening the bank's resilience to potential economic shocks.

Valuation

East West Bancorp, with the current valuation, is traded with a clear premium compared to the historical averages and direct competitors. The current P/E is reaching 12.5x, which is over 20% above the three-year historical average, suggesting that the share price has grown more quickly than the income. P/B ratio is at 1.9x, which is well above the regional bank sector average, which is around 1.3x.

Compared to Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL), whose P/E ratio is at 9.4, EWBC is more expensive, though it is justified by the better asset quality and a lower net write-off level, which is reaching 0.09%, when WAL's ratio is at 0.18%. EWBC's efficiency ratio at 36.2% remains one of the best in the industry, while First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA) has the same ratio at 51.4%. This shows that EWBC's operational structure is a lot more efficient than FCNCA's in generating revenues.

ROE is reaching 16%, surpassing Cullen/Frost Bankers (CFR) with a 15.1% ratio. Even though EWBC is demonstrating a higher profitability, the dividend yield of 2.56% is lower than that of CFR at 3.1%, which shows an orientation to reinvesting capital rather than passive revenues. In my view, the high valuation means that any profit forecast discrepancy could cause a rapid share price correction because the safety margin is currently very minimal.

Risks

The main East West Bancorp risk in 2026 remains directly tied to geopolitical tension between the US and China, which directly affects the bank's main client segment and international operations. Since the bank operates as a financial bridge between these regions, any new trading tariffs or restrictions on capital movement directly lower the loan demand and the foreign exchange revenues, which in 2026 Q1 added to the record number of $99 million in fee income.

Another critical risk is the commercial real estate portfolio, which is reaching $20.2 billion, or 35% of the whole loan portfolio. Even though the bank consciously slowed the CRE growth to 3%, the office and retail segments remain sensitive due to the prolonged high-interest rate environment, which could force the bank to increase provisions to cover losses from the current low level.

The net interest margin, which reached 3.49%, is facing negative pressure due to the deposit rotation. Deposit costs dropped to 2.10% over the year, and the proportion of non-interest-bearing deposits shrank from 32% to 28%, which shows a decreasing base of cheap funds and potential sensitivity to a higher deposit beta ratio if competition for deposits increases.

If the Federal Reserve Bank starts to lower interest rates, the bank's revenues from the variable interest loans will drop more quickly than it will be possible to reduce deposit costs, which would create margin pressure.

Also, there is a concentration risk in the specific industries, such as the entertainment business and renewable energy, where credit quality is dependent on the country‘s subsidies and regulatory environment stability. Any macroeconomic slowdown in both of the regions at the same time would cause a systemic risk, which the bank can‘t fully diversify due to its niche business strategy.

Thesis summary

2026 Q1 results just confirmed that the business is currently operating at a high level. However, the market already sees that, and the current share price, in my opinion, has already priced in a big part of these advantages. The valuation is not cheap, and the safety margin has become limited. This means that even a small disappointment with margins, deposit costs, quality of commercial real estate, or the US and China's relationship could cause a decline in share price.

In my view, this is not a bad company; quite the opposite, it's a very strong bank, though not every strong company automatically means an attractive investment at any price.
